questions.question: Explain the concept of locality of reference and its two types. How does it relate to cache memory effectiveness?
keyPoints: Define locality of reference as the tendency of the processor to access the same memory locations repeatedly
Explain temporal locality: recently accessed items likely accessed again
Explain spatial locality: nearby locations likely accessed soon
Relate to cache: cache works because programs exhibit locality
Higher locality leads to higher cache hit rates and better performance

questions.question: Differentiate between write-through and write-back cache policies. Mention their advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: Write-through: writes to both cache and main memory simultaneously
Write-back: writes to cache first, main memory updated on eviction
Write-through advantages: simple, data consistency guaranteed
Write-through disadvantages: slower writes, uses more memory bandwidth
Write-back advantages: faster writes, less memory traffic
Write-back disadvantages: complex, potential data inconsistency

questions.question: Describe the three main cache mapping techniques with their advantages and limitations.
keyPoints: Direct mapping: each block mapped to one cache line using modulo operation
Advantages: simple hardware, inexpensive
Limitations: fixed mapping can cause thrashing
Fully associative: any block can go anywhere
Advantages: best hit rate, flexibility
Limitations: expensive hardware for parallel search
Set-associative: combination of direct and associative
Advantages: compromise between cost and performance
Limitations: slightly more complex than direct mapping

questions.question: Calculate the average memory access time for a cache system with: Hit time = 2 cycles, Miss rate = 8%, Miss penalty = 50 cycles. Also explain what this calculation signifies.
keyPoints: Formula: AMAT = Hit time + (Miss rate × Miss penalty)
Calculation: 2 + (0.08 × 50) = 2 + 4 = 6 cycles
This represents the effective memory access time considering both cache hits and misses
Lower AMAT indicates better cache performance
Shows trade-off between cache speed and memory latency

questions.question: What is cache coherence problem in multiprocessor systems? How is it typically handled?
keyPoints: Cache coherence: ensuring multiple caches have consistent data
Problem: when one processor updates data in its cache, other processors may have stale copies
Snooping protocols: each cache monitors bus transactions
Write-invalidate protocol: invalidates copies in other caches on write
Write-update protocol: updates copies in other caches on write
Hardware solutions like MESI protocol

questions.question: Explain LRU replacement policy in cache memory. Why is it commonly used despite requiring additional hardware?
keyPoints: LRU stands for Least Recently Used
Replaces the block that has not been accessed for the longest time
Assumes recently used blocks will be used again (temporal locality)
Requires hardware to track the order of block access
Commonly used because it closely approximates optimal replacement
Provides good hit rates for most practical workloads
Hardware can be implemented with counters or stack algorithms

questions.question: What are the differences between L1, L2, and L3 caches in terms of size, speed, and location?
keyPoints: L1 cache: smallest (16-64KB), fastest (1-2 cycle access), on CPU chip
L2 cache: larger (256KB-2MB), slower (10-20 cycles), often on same chip or separate
L3 cache: largest (4MB-64MB), slowest (30-50 cycles), shared among cores
Trade-off: smaller caches are faster but have lower capacity
Modern processors use inclusive or exclusive cache hierarchies
L3 often acts as a victim cache for L2

questions.question: A computer system has a cache with the following specifications: Cache size = 16KB, Block size = 64 bytes, Associativity = 2-way. Calculate: (a) Number of cache lines, (b) Number of sets, (c) Number of bits for offset, index, and tag fields in the address. Assume a 32-bit address.
keyPoints: Cache lines = Cache size / Block size = 16384 / 64 = 256 lines
Sets = Cache lines / Associativity = 256 / 2 = 128 sets
Offset bits = log2(Block size) = log2(64) = 6 bits
Index bits = log2(Number of sets) = log2(128) = 7 bits
Tag bits = Address bits - Offset bits - Index bits = 32 - 6 - 7 = 19 bits
Verification: 6 + 7 + 19 = 32 bits

questions.question: Explain the sequence of events when the CPU requests data that is not present in the cache (cache miss). Discuss both fetch and write scenarios.
keyPoints: Read miss scenario: CPU requests data not in cache
Cache checks valid bits and tags, determines miss
Cache controller fetches block from main memory
Entire block (e.g., 64 bytes) is loaded into cache
Requested data is sent to CPU while block is being loaded
CPU may stall or execute other instructions during fetch
Write miss scenario: CPU writes to a non-cached location
Write-allocate: fetch block first, then update in cache
No-writeallocate: write directly to main memory, bypassing cache
Block replacement may be needed if cache is full
Dirty bit handling in write-back policy

questions.question: Compare and contrast direct-mapped, set-associative, and fully associative cache mapping techniques. Provide a detailed analysis of their hardware complexity, hit rate, and suitability for different applications.
keyPoints: Direct-mapped: simple hardware, one location per block, high conflict miss potential
Set-associative: N-way set (e.g., 4-way), reduces conflicts, moderate hardware
Fully associative: any location, best hit rate, complex expensive hardware
Hardware complexity: direct simplest, set-associative moderate, fully complex
Hit rate: fully best, set-associative good, direct varies with access patterns
Speed: direct fastest lookup, set-associative moderate, fully slowest
Suitability: direct for cost-sensitive, set-associative for general purpose, fully for specialized applications
Trade-offs involve hit rate vs hardware cost vs access time

questions.question: Discuss the various factors that affect cache performance. How can cache miss rate be reduced? Explain with examples.
keyPoints: Factors affecting performance: cache size, block size, associativity, replacement policy
Cache size: larger reduces capacity misses but increases access time
Block size: larger exploits spatial locality but increases miss penalty
Associativity: higher reduces conflict misses but increases lookup time
Replacement policy: LRU better than FIFO but needs more hardware
Write policy impacts performance for write-intensive workloads
Miss rate reduction techniques: increasing cache size, optimizing block size
Using appropriate associativity for the workload
Software optimizations: loop tiling, array padding to reduce conflicts
Hardware optimizations: prefetching, victim caches, non-blocking caches
